caveats: a: No source measures wage-weighted task exposure specifically for NAICS 541820 LMM agencies.
a: The cited AI evidence is a leader survey and describes potential task efficiency, not realized labor removal.
rho: Expected task efficiency is not equivalent to operational implementation or avoidable hiring.
rho: Regulated public affairs and crisis work may have lower adoption than general commercial PR.
e: The Census definition does not publish the revenue share of political consulting or one-off work.
e: Eligibility is conditional on the injected LMM-band count and is not an establishment-count estimate.
s5: Intentions are not completed transfers and Gallup is not industry specific.
s5: PRovoke's identified-deal count has unknown geographic coverage, firm-size coverage, and denominator.
q: The pricing survey covers branding, creative, digital, marketing, PR, social-media, and web agencies rather than PR alone.
q: Relationship tenure does not prove that an agency retains AI-created savings at renewal.
d5: Occupational employment is not PR-agency revenue or constant-quality demand.
d5: Only 13% of public-relations specialists worked in the broader advertising, public relations, and related-services industry in the cited BLS profile.
o: No source measures the share of NAICS 541820 service quantity that will remain operator supplied after five years.
o: Crisis, public-affairs, and commodity-content engagements can have materially different substitution rates.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External organizations buy advisory and execution services through agency-of-record retainers, monthly fees, hourly engagements, or projects; the screen retains only firms with recurring or repeat service revenue and roughly $1–10M normalized EBITDA.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541820 explicitly combines public relations, lobbying, and political consulting. Election-cycle political consulting has a materially different buyer, timing, and repeat-revenue pattern, so it is excluded to make the recurring outsourced-service screen coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Election-cycle political campaign consulting, isolated one-off campaigns, captive communications teams, media buying, advertising placement, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent commercial, nonprofit, and public-affairs agencies providing repeat outsourced reputation, earned-media, stakeholder, crisis-communications, and lobbying counsel to external clients.

caveats: No source measures wage-weighted task exposure specifically for NAICS 541820 LMM agencies.
The cited AI evidence is a leader survey and describes potential task efficiency, not realized labor removal.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from sampled LMM PR agencies, with time-sheet roles and pre/post-AI workflow measurements, would directly measure exposed labor by occupation.
a.rationale: BLS lists writing releases and speeches, responding to media, social-media monitoring, opinion evaluation, and promotion evaluation among core PR-specialist duties (S2). PR leaders report AI use for content, research, pitches, memos, summaries, distribution, and administration (S3). Those activities create substantial wage-weighted exposure, but relationship building, message judgment, executive counsel, and crisis decisions remain human work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects U.S. public-relations-specialist employment to rise 5% from 315,900 in 2024 to 331,000 in 2034 (S2). Converting that ten-year occupational change to a five-year ratio gives 1.025 as a proxy for constant-quality demand for this lens's service basket. It is likely upward-biased for outsourced agency demand because the occupation spans in-house, government, education, and other employers; the operator-elimination risk is separately represented by o.
caveats: Occupational employment is not PR-agency revenue or constant-quality demand.
Only 13% of public-relations specialists worked in the broader advertising, public relations, and related-services industry in the cited BLS profile.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar forecast for U.S. outsourced PR-agency fees by service line, with spend separated from in-house communications employment and political-cycle work, would materially improve this primitive.
d5.rationale: The proxy supports modest quantity growth rather than contraction: BLS attributes continued need to community outreach, customer relations, rapid information spread, and social-media use (S2). It does not establish a direct industry revenue or outsourced-demand forecast, so the range accommodates weaker demand and substitution.

caveats: The Census definition does not publish the revenue share of political consulting or one-off work.
Eligibility is conditional on the injected LMM-band count and is not an establishment-count estimate.
e.change_evidence: A NAICS 541820 firm-level revenue survey separating retainer, repeat, project, lobbying, and political-campaign revenue, cross-tabulated by EBITDA, would identify the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition covers agencies designing and implementing PR campaigns and also includes lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting (S1). The included recurring external-client lens fits many agencies using retainers or repeat accounts, but election-cycle political work and isolated campaign work do not satisfy it; the estimate reflects that mixed code without treating every establishment as eligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541820 ($9,020,215,177) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541820 ($16,903,608,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $271,021 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541820); class <5: 6,209 firms, avg revenue/firm $468,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,381 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 927 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,829,861 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $286,373 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 551 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,315,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $518,871 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,294,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,611,030 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,745,397 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,063,655 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,824,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,962,036 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 421

caveats: No source measures the share of NAICS 541820 service quantity that will remain operator supplied after five years.
Crisis, public-affairs, and commodity-content engagements can have materially different substitution rates.
o.change_evidence: Client procurement data comparing outsourced PR spend, in-house communications headcount, and software substitution by service line over multiple renewal cycles would directly test operator dependence.
o.rationale: BLS describes PR work requiring communication with media and the public, client representation, judgment on sensitive issues, speaking, and writing (S2). These accountability-intensive activities support continued operator demand, while S3 reports AI support for content, research, pitching, summaries, distribution, and administrative work and notes that in-house teams can use AI for duties formerly outsourced. The estimate therefore retains a majority of the current basket while recognizing software and self-service displacement.

caveats: The pricing survey covers branding, creative, digital, marketing, PR, social-media, and web agencies rather than PR alone.
Relationship tenure does not prove that an agency retains AI-created savings at renewal.
q.change_evidence: LMM PR-agency contract and renewal data showing billing method, AI-enabled hour reduction, rate resets, retained margin, and client churn would directly measure benefit retention.
q.rationale: Time-based billing exposes labor savings to client repricing: a PR-industry report says most PR agencies generate revenue from time and cited 1,685 billed hours per employee in 2022 (S3). In a broader agency survey, hourly billing was 28%, productized or subscription packages 28%, retainers 10%, and projects 25% (S7). Countervailing retention comes from AOR relationships: the ANA and 4As report roughly seven-year average client-agency tenure, with public relations among the longer-tenure agency types and a majority of top-ten clients in AOR or retainer relationships (S4).

caveats: Expected task efficiency is not equivalent to operational implementation or avoidable hiring.
Regulated public affairs and crisis work may have lower adoption than general commercial PR.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year adoption and staffing data from PR agency benchmarking surveys, plus agency interviews documenting which AI-supported workflows reduce hiring or hours after quality review, would materially revise this estimate.
rho.rationale: The task set in S2 includes material that can be drafted, researched, monitored, or summarized with AI, and 88% of PR leaders expected positive speed and efficiency effects in S3. Implementation is limited by client approvals, factual accuracy, confidentiality, brand voice, sensitive stakeholder relationships, and the need for experienced judgment in crises.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions among U.S. employer-business owners, not completed qualifying transfers of LMM public-relations agencies: 22% reported an intended sale, transfer, or public offering (S5). The base scales that intention rate down for uncompleted plans, closures, and owner-dependent firms; it is likely upward-biased relative to completed qualifying transfers. PRovoke's count of 80 identified PR acquisitions in 2025 (S6) confirms an active sector market but does not supply a firm denominator or LMM share.
caveats: Intentions are not completed transfers and Gallup is not industry specific.
PRovoke's identified-deal count has unknown geographic coverage, firm-size coverage, and denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A private-company transaction database matched to NAICS 541820, EBITDA, buyer type, and control-transfer status, together with an owner-age and succession survey of LMM agencies, would directly measure the probability.
s5.rationale: Owner succession is relevant because 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people at least 55 and employer owners report five-year transfer intentions (S5). Sector transaction activity is visible, although 2025 PR acquisition volume declined and larger agencies formed a higher share of completed deals (S6), supporting a cautious conversion from intent to transfer.

principal_driver: A large share of recurring PR workflow is information production and coordination that can be AI-assisted, while reputation-sensitive advisory work preserves an operator role.
principal_weakness: Hourly and project pricing can transfer AI productivity to clients, and the code's political and one-off work complicates both recurring-revenue eligibility and transferability.
